26: 27: Wagtail is an open source content management system built on Django, with a strong community and commercial support. It's focused on user experience, and offers precise control for designers and developers. 28: 29: ![Wagtail screenshot](https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/gh/wagtail/wagtail@main/.github/wagtail-screenshot-with-browser.png) 30: 31: ### 🔥 Features 32: 33: - A fast, attractive interface for authors 34: - Complete control over front-end design and structure 35: - Scales to millions of pages and thousands of editors 36: - Fast out of the box, cache-friendly when you need it 37: - Content API for 'headless' sites with decoupled front-end 38: - Runs on a Raspberry Pi or a multi-datacenter cloud platform 39: - StreamField encourages flexible content without compromising structure 40: - Powerful, integrated search, using Elasticsearch or PostgreSQL 41: - Excellent support for images and embedded content 42: - Multi-site and multi-language ready 43: - Embraces and extends Django 44: 45: Find out more at [wagtail.org](https://wagtail.org/). 46: 47: ### 👉 Getting started 48: 49: Wagtail works with [Python 3](https://www.python.org/downloads/), on any platform. 50: 51: To get started with using Wagtail, run the following in a [virtual environment](https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/venv.html): 52: 53: ![Installing Wagtail](.github/install-animation.gif) 54: 55: ```sh 56: pip install wagtail 57: wagtail start mysite 58: cd mysite 59: pip install -r requirements.txt 60: python manage.py migrate 61: python manage.py createsuperuser 62: python manage.py runserver 63: ``` 64: 65: For detailed installation and setup docs, see [the getting started tutorial](https://docs.wagtail.org/en/stable/getting_started/tutorial.html). 66: 67: ### 👨‍👩‍👧‍👦 Who’s using it? Add your own Wagtail site to [madewithwagtail.org](https://madewithwagtail.org). 70: 71: ### 📖 Documentation 72: 73: [docs.wagtail.org](https://docs.wagtail.org/) is the full reference for Wagtail, and includes guides for developers, designers and editors, alongside [release notes](https://docs.wagtail.org/en/stable/releases/) and our [roadmap](https://wagtail.org/roadmap/). 74: 75: For those who are **new to Wagtail**, the [Zen of Wagtail](https://docs.wagtail.org/en/stable/getting_started/the_zen_of_wagtail.html) will help you understand what Wagtail is, and what Wagtail is _not_. 76: 77: **For developers** who are ready to jump in to their first Wagtail website the [Getting Started Tutorial](https://docs.wagtail.org/en/stable/getting_started/tutorial.html) will guide you through creating and editing your first page. 78: 79: **Do you have an existing Django project?** The [Wagtail Integration documentation](https://docs.wagtail.org/en/stable/getting_started/integrating_into_django.html) is the best place to start. 80: 81: ### 📌 Compatibility 82: 83: _(If you are reading this on GitHub, the details here may not be indicative of the current released version - please see [Compatible Django / Python versions](https://docs.wagtail.org/en/stable/releases/upgrading.html#compatible-django-python-versions) in the Wagtail documentation.)_ 84: 85: Wagtail supports: 86: 87: - Django 5.2.x and 6.0.x 88: - Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12, 3.13, and 3.14 89: - PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB and SQLite (with JSON1) as database backends 90: 91: [Previous versions of Wagtail](https://docs.wagtail.org/en/stable/releases/upgrading.html#compatible-django-python-versions) additionally supported Python 2.7, 3.8 and earlier Django versions. 92: 93: --- 94: 95: ### 📢 Community Support 96: 97: There is an active community of Wagtail users and developers responding to questions on [Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/wagtail). When posting questions, please read Stack Overflow's advice on [how to ask questions](https://stackoverflow.com/help/how-to-ask) and remember to tag your question "wagtail". 98: 99: For topics and discussions that do not fit Stack Overflow's question and answer format we have a [Slack workspace](https://github.com/wagtail/wagtail/wiki/Slack). Please respect the time and effort of volunteers by not asking the same question in multiple places. 100: 101: [![Join slack community](.github/join-slack-community.png)](https://github.com/wagtail/wagtail/wiki/Slack) 102: 103: Our [GitHub discussion boards](https://github.com/wagtail/wagtail/discussions) are open for sharing ideas and plans for the Wagtail project. 104: 105: We maintain a curated list of third party packages, articles and other resources at [Awesome Wagtail](https://github.com/springload/awesome-wagtail). 106: 107: ### 🧑‍💼 Commercial Support 108: 109: Wagtail is sponsored by [Torchbox](https://torchbox.com/). See also [madewithwagtail.org/developers/](https://madewithwagtail.org/developers/) for expert Wagtail developers around the world. 110: 111: ### 🔐 Security 112: 113: We take the security of Wagtail, and related packages we maintain, seriously. If you have found a security issue with any of our projects please email us at [security@wagtail.org](mailto:security@wagtail.org) so we can work together to find and patch the issue. 592:     @staticmethod
593:     def _slug_is_available(slug, parent_page, page=None):
594:         """
595:         Determine whether the given slug is available for use on a child page of
596:         parent_page. If 'page' is passed, the slug is intended for use on that page
597:         (and so it will be excluded from the duplicate check).
598:         """
599:         if parent_page is None:
600:             # the root page's slug can be whatever it likes...
601:             return True
602: 
603:         siblings = parent_page.get_children()
604:         if page:
605:             siblings = siblings.not_page(page)
606: 
607:         return not siblings.filter(slug=slug).exists()

615:             # try with incrementing suffix until we find a slug which is available
616:             suffix += 1

## Step 1: Orient in the codebase

This is Wagtail, an open source Django CMS (content management system) focused on user experience and flexible content modeling. The project structure is organized into functional modules:

- **`wagtail/models/`** - Core data models (pages, sites, revisions, workflows, etc.)

- **`wagtail/rich_text/`** - Rich text and streamfield functionality
- **`wagtail/templatetags/`** - Django template tags
- **`wagtail/tests/`** - Comprehensive test suite

### **CRITICAL RISK - Will Break Loudly** (immediate functional failure)

1. **`wagtail/models/pages.py:306`** (content_type ForeignKey to ContentType)
   - **Dependency type**: Schema field change - page.content_typeFK must reference existing ContentType
   - **Risk**: If content_type field is moved to satellite model, all queries filtering by content_type break
   - **Impact**: All Page queries using content_type filter, get_by_natural_key, content_type lookups fail
   - **Coupling**: Tight - schema-level dependency

2. **`wagtail/urls.py`** (implicit via serve())  
   - **Flow**: serve() → Page.route() → find_for_request() → route_for_request()
   - **Dependency type**: Core routing - URL path resolution depends on Page.url_path
   - **Risk**: If url_path field removed, all public-facing page serving fails
   - **Impact**: Visitor-facing pages return 404, site completely broken
   - **Coupling**: Tight - runtime routing

3. **`wagtail/models/sites.py:90`** (Site.root_page ForeignKey)
   - **Dependency type**: Schema field - Site.root_page FK to Page
   - **Risk**: If root_page field removed from Site, all site root lookups break

4. **`wagtail/admin/views/chooser.py:235`**, **440**, **512** (BrowseView, SearchView, ChosenMultipleView)

## Summary of Dependencies by Risk Level

### CRITICAL - Must Be Checked First
1. **Schema fields**: content_type, owner, alias_of, url_path, path, depth, numchild
2. **Generic ForeignKeys**: Revision.content_object, WorkflowState.page
3. **Template tags**: slugurl(), test_page_is_public()
4. **Core serving**: serve(), Page.route(), find_for_request()
5. **Site relationship**: Site.root_page

### HIGH - Subsystem-Level Check
6. **Admin UI**: All admin views querying Page or related models
7. **Actions**: move_page, copy_page, publish_page_revision, unpublish_page, delete_page
8. **Relations**: GroupPagePermission, PageViewRestriction, WorkflowPage, PageLogEntry, PageSubscription page FKs
9. **Extensions**: AbstractFormSubmission.form_page, Redirect.redirect_page, SearchPromotion.page

### MEDIUM - Hidden Interactions
10. **Query conveniences**: PageQuerySet, TreeQuerySet methods
11. **Signal handlers**: after_create_page, purge_page_from_cache
12. **Templates**: template tags and filters using Page
13. **Management commands**: fixtree, move_pages, purge_revisions

### LOW - Test/Example Only
14. **Tests**: All test files referencing Page
15. **Examples**: Project template, demosite, testapp models

## Key Takeaways for the PR

1. **Page model is central to Wagtail** - Every core feature (admin, serving, workflows, forms, redirects, search) depends on it
2. **Tree mechanics are critical** - path, depth, numchild fields used by treebeard for Page hierarchy
3. **Publishing state is widely used** - live, has_unpublished_changes, latest_revision fields referenced by admin, serving, and API
4. **Generic relations make backwards dependencies** - Revision, WorkflowState, Comment, PageViewRestriction, PageSubscription all reference Page via GenericRelation or ForeignKey
5. **Admin UI is tightly coupled** - 50+ admin views directly query Page or related models
6. **Migrations must be updated** - Old migrations may break if Page model relationships change
7. **API consumers will break** - REST API endpoints expose Page model fields directly
8. **Template tags break silently** - {% slugurl %}, test_page_is_public() fail if Page lookup changes
